revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''鋸歯文'''（きょしもん）とは、[[三角形]]を連鎖して表現した[[幾何学]][[文様]]の1つ。[[鋸]]の歯状に並べたように...」
revision.text:
'''鋸歯文'''（きょしもん）とは、[[三角形]]を連鎖して表現した[[幾何学]][[文様]]の1つ。[[鋸]]の歯状に並べたように見えることから命名された<ref name=sakadume>坂詰『国史大辞典』「鋸歯文」</ref><ref name=kanou>加納『日本歴史大事典』「鋸歯文」</ref>。
[[中国]]の[[漢]]代の[[青銅器]]などにみられるため、日本には中国から渡来した可能性がある<ref name=sakadume/>。一方で[[縄文式土器]]の段階で既に存在していたとする指摘も存在する<ref name=kanou/>。
[[弥生時代]]以降になると様々な文物に用いられ、[[弥生式土器]]や[[銅鐸]]、[[銅鏡]]、[[埴輪]]、[[瓦]]、[[古墳]]の壁面装飾などに見られる<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。また、鋸歯文の中に平行線を刻んだり、彩色を施した文様も確認されている<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *坂詰秀一「鋸歯文」（『国史大辞典 4』（吉川弘文館、1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3） *加納俊介「鋸歯文」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6）
{{DEFAULTSORT:きよしもん}} [[Category:模様]]

The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</re..."
revision.text:
'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
[[Category:Patterns]] [[ja:鋸歯文]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Sawtooth designs]] to [[Sawtooth patterns]]
revision.text:
'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
[[Category:Patterns]] [[ja:鋸歯文]]

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
[[Category:Patterns]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:鋸歯文]]

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
[[Category:Patterns]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:New pages]][[ja:鋸歯文]]

The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

revision.text:
'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

revision.text:
'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

5 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:鋸歯文 up to rev 91029056
revision.text:
'''鋸歯文'''（きょしもん）とは、[[三角形]]を連鎖して表現した[[幾何学文様]]の1つ。[[鋸]]の歯状に並べたように見えることから命名された<ref name=sakadume>坂詰『国史大辞典』「鋸歯文」</ref><ref name=kanou>加納『日本歴史大事典』「鋸歯文」</ref>。
[[中国]]の[[漢]]代の[[青銅器]]などにみられるため、日本には中国から渡来した可能性がある<ref name=sakadume/>。一方で[[縄文土器]]の段階で既に存在していたとする指摘も存在する<ref name=kanou/>。
[[弥生時代]]以降になると様々な文物に用いられ、[[弥生土器]]や[[銅鐸]]、[[銅鏡]]、[[埴輪]]、[[瓦]]、[[古墳]]の壁面装飾などに見られる<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。また、鋸歯文の中に平行線を刻んだり、彩色を施した文様も確認されている<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *坂詰秀一「鋸歯文」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 4』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 *加納俊介「鋸歯文」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]] 2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6
{{DEFAULTSORT:きよしもん}} [[Category:模様]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[鋸歯文]] to [[Sawtooth patterns]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''鋸歯文'''（きょしもん）とは、[[三角形]]を連鎖して表現した[[幾何学文様]]の1つ。[[鋸]]の歯状に並べたように見えることから命名された<ref name=sakadume>坂詰『国史大辞典』「鋸歯文」</ref><ref name=kanou>加納『日本歴史大事典』「鋸歯文」</ref>。
[[中国]]の[[漢]]代の[[青銅器]]などにみられるため、日本には中国から渡来した可能性がある<ref name=sakadume/>。一方で[[縄文土器]]の段階で既に存在していたとする指摘も存在する<ref name=kanou/>。
[[弥生時代]]以降になると様々な文物に用いられ、[[弥生土器]]や[[銅鐸]]、[[銅鏡]]、[[埴輪]]、[[瓦]]、[[古墳]]の壁面装飾などに見られる<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。また、鋸歯文の中に平行線を刻んだり、彩色を施した文様も確認されている<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *坂詰秀一「鋸歯文」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 4』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 *加納俊介「鋸歯文」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]] 2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6
{{DEFAULTSORT:きよしもん}} [[Category:模様]]

'''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様|qq=draft|qq=draft}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|ja|弥生土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|dotaku|ja|銅鐸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
{{translated page|ja|鋸歯文|version=91029056|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Patterns]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sawtooth patterns]] [[Category:New]] }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鋸歯文]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q56351580]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/geometric pattern|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 幾何学模様|1=geometric pattern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28068264}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work=[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)]] Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze vessels|qq=|1=bronze object|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4390114}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]].<ref name=kanou/>
From the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as [[:en:Yayoi pottery|Yayoi pottery]], [[:en:Dōtaku|dotaku]], [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]], [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], tiles, and the wall decorations of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]].<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)]]* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.

The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work=[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)]] Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze vessels|1=bronze object|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4390114}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]].<ref name=kanou/>
From the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as [[:en:Yayoi pottery|Yayoi pottery]], [[:en:Dōtaku|dotaku]], [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]], [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], tiles, and the wall decorations of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]].<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)]]* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * Kanou, Shunsuke. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}}* Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
{{translated page|ja|鋸歯文|version=91029056|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]]
}}

From the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as [[:en:Yayoi pottery|Yayoi pottery]], [[:en:Dōtaku|dotaku]], [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]], [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], tiles, and the wall decorations of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]].<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鋸歯文]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鋸歯文]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鋸歯文]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q56351580]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sawtooth Pattern''' (鋸歯文, Kyoshimon) is a type of {{ill|geometric pattern|ja|幾何学文様|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/geometric pattern|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 幾何学模様|1=geometric pattern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28068264}} characterized by a series of interconnected triangles. The name derives from its resemblance to the teeth of a saw.<ref name=sakadume>{{Cite book|author=Sakazume, Shuichi|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|en|Kokushi Daijiten|WD=Q11420603|lt=Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)}} Vol. 4|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|year=1984|isbn=978-4-642-00504-3}}</ref><ref name=kanou>{{Cite book|author=Kanou, Shunsuke|title=Sawtooth Pattern|work={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1|publisher=Shogakukan|year=2000|isbn=978-4-095-23001-6}}</ref>
This pattern is observed on {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}} {{ill|bronze vessels|ja|青銅器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze vessels|1=bronze object|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4390114}} in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, suggesting it may have been introduced to Japan from China.<ref name=sakadume/> However, some scholars argue it already existed during the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}.<ref name=kanou/>
From the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|en|Yayoi pottery|WD=Q11487461|lt=Yayoi pottery}}, {{ill|Dōtaku|en|Dōtaku|WD=Q2312868|lt=dotaku}}, {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirrors}}, {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Sakazume, Shuichi. "Sawtooth Pattern." In *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|en|Kokushi Daijiten|WD=Q11420603|lt=Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)}}* Vol. 4, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1984. From the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} onward, the sawtooth pattern was widely applied to various cultural artifacts, such as {{ill|Yayoi pottery|en|Yayoi pottery|WD=Q11487461|lt=Yayoi pottery|1=Yayoi pottery}}, {{ill|Dōtaku|en|Dōtaku|WD=Q2312868|lt=dotaku|1=Dōtaku}}, {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirrors|1=Bronze mirror}}, {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}}, tiles, and the wall decorations of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/> Additionally, some variations of the sawtooth pattern include parallel lines engraved within the design or added coloration.<ref name=sakadume/><ref name=kanou/>
